reply:
This is a genuinely interesting theological question that has been debated seriously throughout Christian history.

## Different Positions

**Eternal existence (Nicene/Orthodox Christianity)**
- The Council of Nicaea (325 AD) declared Jesus/the Son was "begotten, not made" and "of the same substance" as the Father
- He existed eternally alongside the Father, never having a beginning
- John 1:1 supports this: "In the beginning was the Word"
- John 8:58: "Before Abraham was, I am"

**Created at some point (Arianism)**
- Arius argued the Son was the Father's first creation, existing before the physical world but having a definite beginning
- "There was a time when he was not"
- Proverbs 8:22 was cited as supporting this
- This view was declared heresy at Nicaea but remained influential

**Modern groups holding similar views**
- Jehovah's Witnesses teach Jesus was the first created being (Michael the Archangel)
- Some Latter-day Saints have different frameworks entirely

## The Core Tension
The debate hinges on how to interpret passages like Colossians 1:15 ("firstborn of all creation") - does "firstborn" mean first created, or a title of preeminence?

Most mainstream Christianity today follows the Nicene position of eternal existence.

What angle interests you most?
